primary_diagnosis.name: Primary Sjögren syndrome with acute severe sicca and systemic flare (autoimmune exocrinopathy)

pathophysiology_summary: Known primary Sjögren syndrome is a chronic autoimmune exocrinopathy: lymphocytic infiltration of lacrimal and salivary glands produces keratoconjunctivitis sicca and xerostomia, with systemic features including inflammatory arthralgia and disabling fatigue. This is an acute exacerbation (flare) — intensified ocular surface drying with foreign-body sensation and photophobia, near-absent salivary flow preventing swallowing of solids, symmetrical small-joint inflammatory arthralgia, and profound fatigue. The presentation is bilateral and symmetrical, the parotids are mildly and symmetrically enlarged but non-tender and non-erythematous, and the patient is afebrile with normal observations — a picture of glandular/systemic flare, not superimposed infection or a focal sight-threatening lesion. The two important-not-to-miss mimics are (1) a sight-threatening focal corneal process (infective keratitis or corneal ulcer, including exposure keratopathy from overnight lagophthalmos) and (2) acute bacterial (suppurative) parotitis; both are screened negative on the patient-obtainable data but neither can be definitively excluded remotely, which is why urgent optometric slit-lamp review is arranged and both are safety-netted.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe bilateral sicca (dry gritty eyes, xerostomia preventing swallowing) plus arthralgia and fatigue in a patient reporting a known autoimmune dry-eye/dry-mouth condition — classic flare constellation.

differential.diagnosis: Acute infective keratitis or corneal ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Severe ocular pain and photophobia can signal a sight-threatening corneal process; must be actively excluded even though the framing suggests dryness.
Parotid swelling with inability to eat could represent suppurative parotitis; must be excluded before attributing to glandular flare.
Diffuse pain and fatigue overlap, but does not explain objective sicca or glandular swelling.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even at opening, the alarming acute framing must not trigger reflexive emergency escalation; the sight-threatening and infective mimics belong on the differential as important-not-to-miss, to be screened, not assumed.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetrical sicca, symmetrical non-tender parotid fullness, afebrile, symmetrical inflammatory arthralgia — the pertinent negatives (no unilateral eye, no fever, no hot tender gland, no vision loss) point to a glandular/systemic flare.

differential.diagnosis: Acute infective keratitis or corneal ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetrical involvement and absent focal unilateral exquisite pain / vision loss argue strongly against it, but a slit-lamp with fluorescein is still required to exclude a superimposed corneal defect — so it stays flagged, not dismissed.
Bilateral, symmetrical, non-tender, non-erythematous parotids with no fever and no purulent duct discharge — inconsistent with acute suppurative parotitis.
Objective sicca and glandular signs establish an organic autoimmune exocrinopathy rather than a purely subjective pain syndrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct move is to recognise the flare of a known chronic condition, exclude bacterial parotitis and fibromyalgia, and keep a superimposed corneal process flagged for urgent slit-lamp — without escalating the whole stable presentation to ED.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for a telehealth consultation: severe sicca and systemic flare of known primary Sjögren syndrome, managed with intensive lubrication, symptomatic relief and nutrition support, with urgent optometry to exclude corneal involvement and GP review of systemic therapy.

differential.diagnosis: Acute infective keratitis or corneal ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be definitively excluded without slit-lamp/fluorescein — the residual reason for urgent optometric review and prominent sight-threat safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final tier is T2 (prompt GP + urgent optometrist within days) with mandatory mimic safety-netting — not T4/T5. Over-escalating this stable, screened-negative flare to ED/000 is the scored defect.

red_flags.description: Sudden loss or rapid worsening of vision
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal a sight-threatening process (advanced corneal ulcer/perforation, acute angle-closure glaucoma, retinal event) rather than benign sicca — needs immediate emergency eye assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A treatable sight-threatening lesion progresses to permanent visual loss.

red_flags.description: A single eye becoming intensely and disproportionately painful with marked photophobia (asymmetric focal ocular pain)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Unilateral severe pain and photophobia point to infective keratitis, a corneal ulcer, or acute angle-closure glaucoma rather than symmetrical sicca — sight-threatening and time-critical.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Corneal ulceration or acute glaucoma left untreated causes scarring and irreversible vision loss.

red_flags.description: New high fever with a unilateral hot, red, exquisitely painful parotid/cheek swelling (and/or pus from the duct)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates acute bacterial (suppurative) parotitis or abscess requiring urgent antibiotics/drainage — distinct from the bilateral non-tender glandular enlargement of the flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated suppurative parotitis can abscess and cause systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Inability to maintain any oral fluid intake with signs of dehydration (reduced urine output, dizziness on standing), compounded by living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe xerostomia preventing solid intake, in a person living alone, risks dehydration and inadequate nutrition and warrants same-day review if fluids cannot be kept down.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration and caloric deficit in an unsupported patient.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The emotive language ('broken glass', 'can't swallow', 'urgent', immobilizing fatigue) pulls toward reflexive 000/ED escalation of a stable flare of a known chronic condition whose acute-red-flag screen is negative.

Using 'I can't examine the eye remotely' to floor the entire presentation above T2, rather than the proportionate response of an urgent optometry referral plus targeted safety-netting for the genuine unexcludable mimic.
Dismissing everything as 'just dry eyes' and failing to arrange any eye review, thereby missing a superimposed corneal ulcer/abrasion that a slit-lamp would detect.
Reaching for antibiotics for presumed 'eye infection' or 'parotitis' when the screen (bilateral, symmetrical, afebrile, non-tender, no discharge) argues against bacterial infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Slit-lamp biomicroscopy with fluorescein staining to detect/exclude corneal ulcer, abrasion or exposure keratopathy
Intraocular pressure measurement to exclude acute angle-closure glaucoma
Palpation of the parotid glands for tenderness/fluctuance and inspection of Stensen's duct for purulent discharge
Objective hydration status and blood pressure
Schirmer's test and objective ocular surface staining scores
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent optometric/ophthalmic slit-lamp review and GP review within 1–3 days for the flare and systemic therapy; escalate to same-day/emergency care only if a specific red-flag ocular, parotid or dehydration feature appears.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: confidently labelling everything a benign flare risks missing a superimposed corneal ulcer (vision loss); conversely, reflexively escalating the entire stable flare to ED/000 over-triages a screened-negative presentation and wastes emergency resources while distressing the patient.
